1: Psalm 45 is the only psalm that is explicitly a wedding song — a composition for a royal marriage. Its superscription calls it shir yedidot ('song of loves/beloved ones') and attributes it to the Sons of Korah for the choirmaster. 'My heart is overflowing with a beautiful theme; I compose my poem for the king' — the psalmist describes his own composition process: the heart overflows (rachash libby davar tov, my heart stirs/bubbles up with a good word) like a spring. The explicit artistic self-description ('my tongue is like the pen of a skilled scribe') is rare in the Psalter and marks this as a piece of conscious literary craft. Hebrews 1:8-9 applies this psalm to Christ.
2: 'You are more handsome than any other man; grace is poured into your lips; therefore God has blessed you forever' — royal praise poetry of the king's physical beauty was a genre in ANE court literature (Egyptian and Mesopotamian royal encomia regularly praised the king's divine appearance). 'Grace is poured into your lips' (hutsak chen bisfatecha) — the king's speech is itself a gift: his words carry persuasive power and covenant authority. 'God has blessed you forever' — the eternal blessing connects the royal person to the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:13-16: 'I will establish your kingdom forever').
3: 'Strap your sword on your thigh, O champion (gibbor), in your glory and splendor' — the transition to the king's military identity. Gibbor (warrior/hero/mighty man) was a key epithet for the ideal king and, in messianic texts, for the coming ruler (Isa 9:6: 'Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God (el gibbor), Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace'). The sword on the thigh was the standard position for a warrior ready for battle. The call to ride forth in 'glory and splendor' (hod vehadar) uses the terminology of divine royalty (cf. Ps 96:6: 'Splendor and majesty are before him').
4: 'Ride out victoriously in your splendor for the sake of truth, humility, and righteousness; let your right hand display awesome deeds' — the king's military campaign is not mere conquest but covenant justice: he rides 'for the sake of truth (emet), humility (anava), and righteousness (tsedek).' These three qualities are the same attributes demanded of the covenant-faithful Israelite in Micah 6:8 ('do justice, love kindness, walk humbly'). The ideal king's military power is morally ordered: his right hand does 'awesome deeds' (nora'ot) in the service of covenant values, not mere imperial ambition.
5: 'Your arrows are sharp, bringing peoples down beneath you; they pierce into the hearts of the king's enemies' — the arrow imagery of divine-warrior literature: the king as archer whose arrows pierce hearts (cf. Ps 7:13; Lam 3:12-13; Isa 49:2: the Servant's mouth 'like a sharp sword' and himself 'like a polished arrow'). The 'peoples' (ammim) falling beneath the royal warrior evokes the enthronement Psalms (Ps 47:3: 'He subdues peoples under us') and the Abrahamic promise of universal blessing/dominion.
6: 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever; the scepter of your kingdom is a righteous scepter' — one of the most theologically significant lines in the Psalter: the king is addressed as 'God' (Elohim). The interpretation is debated: is this hyperbolic address to the human king (as in Egyptian court language where the pharaoh was called divine)? Or is it a genuine divine predication? Hebrews 1:8-9 takes it as the latter, applying the verse directly to the Son: 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever.' The 'righteous scepter' is the instrument of the king's just rule — the scepter as symbol of royal judicial authority.
7: 'You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness; therefore God, your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions' — the moral foundation of the royal anointing: the king's love of righteousness and hatred of wickedness is the basis of the divine choice. The 'oil of gladness' (shemen sason) is the anointing oil applied to kings at coronation (1 Sam 10:1; 16:13; 1 Kgs 1:39) and to the high priest (Lev 21:10), but here expressed as oil of joy — covenant celebration. 'Beyond your companions' (mechavereicha) — the king is set apart from all peers by this divine anointing.
8: 'All your robes are fragrant with myrrh, aloes, and cassia; from ivory palaces the music of strings has made you glad' — myrrh, aloes, and cassia were the costliest imported perfumes in the ancient Near East, used in temple worship (Exod 30:23-25: the anointing oil formula uses myrrh and cassia) and in royal contexts. The 'ivory palaces' (minei-shen) recall Solomon's ivory throne (1 Kgs 10:18) and Ahab's 'ivory house' condemned by Amos (Amos 3:15; 6:4): the wealth of the royal court encoded in architecture. The sensory richness (fragrance + music) paints the wedding scene with maximum luxuriance.
9: 'Daughters of kings are among your ladies of honor; at your right hand stands the queen arrayed in gold of Ophir' — the position of the queen 'at your right hand' is the position of the honored advocate and royal partner (cf. 1 Kgs 2:19: Bathsheba's seat at Solomon's right hand). 'Gold of Ophir' was the most prized gold in the ancient world (1 Kgs 9:28; 10:11; Job 28:16) — its appearance here signals maximum royal wealth. The daughters of foreign kings ('ladies of honor') represent the political dimension of royal marriage: diplomatic alliances sealed through marriage.
10: 'Listen, daughter, and pay attention; lean in with your ear: forget your own people and your father's house' — the Psalm's address now turns to the queen/bride. The command to 'forget' her people and father's house echoes the Abrahamic call (Gen 12:1: 'Leave your country, your people, and your father's household') and anticipates the marriage covenant: the bride enters a new loyalty structure. The bride's new identity is defined by her relationship to the king, not her family of origin. The NT application (Eph 5:25-32: the church as bride of Christ) picks up this leaving-and-cleaving pattern.
11: 'Then the king will desire your beauty; because he is your lord, bow down to him' — the instruction to submit to the king as lord (adon) is the covenant submission of the wife to the husband in Israelite marriage law (cf. Gen 18:12: Sarah calls Abraham 'lord'; 1 Pet 3:5-6: Sarah's submission as the pattern for holy wives). The king's desire for the bride's beauty (yit'av hamelech yofyech) reciprocates: submission is within a relationship of mutual desire and covenant honor.
12: 'The daughter of Tyre comes bearing a gift; the wealthy among the nations will seek your favor' — Tyre was the preeminent trading city of the ancient Mediterranean world (cf. Ezek 27-28's extended lament over Tyre; Isa 23). The 'daughter of Tyre' bearing a gift to the wedding represents the wealth of international commerce flowing toward the royal court. The gathering of 'wealthy of the nations' (ashirei am) anticipates the eschatological vision of Ps 22:27-29 and Isa 60:5-7 where the nations' wealth flows to Zion.
13: 'The king's daughter is altogether beautiful within; her garment is interwoven with gold' — 'altogether beautiful within' (kol-kevudah bat-melech penimah, all the glory of the king's daughter is inward) — the queen's beauty is described as interior (penimah, within) before exterior. This sequence (interior then exterior) reflects the covenant principle that inner character precedes outward display. The gold-interwoven garments are the exterior confirmation of the interior glory.
14: 'In embroidered garments she will be led to the king; the maidens, her companions, will follow her and will be brought to you' — the wedding procession: the bride led formally to the king's presence, attended by her companions (bridesmaids). The formal leading (tuval lamelech, she will be brought to the king) uses the same ceremonial language as bringing offerings to the temple — the approach to the king has sacral overtones. The attendant maidens form the cortège that dignifies and witnesses the procession.
15: 'With joy and celebration they will be led in; they will enter the king's palace' — the joy (rinnah vesimchah) characterizes the wedding procession's entrance into the palace. The 'palace' (hekal) is the same word as 'temple' — the royal palace and the divine sanctuary share the same architectural and semantic space in ancient Israelite thinking. The entrance of the bride to the king's palace has symbolic depth: the covenant union of ruler and bride in the royal sanctuary.
16: 'In place of your ancestors will be your sons; you will appoint them as princes throughout all the earth' — the dynastic promise: the sons of this royal marriage will become princes. The language echoes the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:12-13: 'I will raise up your offspring after you ... I will establish his kingdom') and extends it to universal scope: 'princes throughout all the earth' (lesharim bechol-ha'arets). The marriage's fruit is a royal lineage that extends across the known world.
17: 'I will cause your name to be remembered in all generations; therefore the peoples will praise you forever and ever' — the psalmist's own vow and task: to make the king's name perpetually remembered. 'Name remembered' (azkir shemcha) is the covenant memory function: the act of keeping the king's name in circulation through literature, liturgy, and transmission. This verse closes the psalm with the psalmist's own commission: the wedding song is itself an act of covenant memory-making, extending the king's name beyond the moment of the wedding into all future generations.
